title: The Hydrologic Cycle
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Animated landscape showing water moving through evaporation, precipitation, runoff, and groundwater with adjustable solar energy and vegetation.

learningObjectives: Trace the movement of water through the hydrologic cycle and identify key processes
Explain how solar energy drives the water cycle by powering evaporation
Analyze how vegetation cover affects the partitioning of water between transpiration, runoff, and infiltration
Predict how changes to one component of the water cycle affect the entire system
prerequisites: Understanding of the three states of water
Basic knowledge of evaporation and condensation
Familiarity with the concept of a cycle in Earth science

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Animated process visualization with slider-based variable manipulation
keyQuestions: How does solar energy drive the entire water cycle?
What role does vegetation play in the water cycle beyond just using water?
How does deforestation change the balance between runoff and infiltration?
Why is the water cycle described as a closed system at the global level?
commonMisconceptions: Students often omit transpiration from the water cycle, thinking only oceans and lakes contribute to evaporation
Many students think water only moves in one direction through the cycle rather than through multiple simultaneous pathways
Students may believe groundwater is a separate system disconnected from the surface water cycle
assessmentOpportunities: Compare student memory drawings of the water cycle with the simulation to identify gaps in understanding
Evaluate cause-and-effect statements from the slider experiments
Assess predictions about deforestation impacts on downstream water resources
